line_preview: The Standard Error Ratio (SER) Method for Determining Sample Size
The Standard Error Ratio (SER) Method for Determining Sample Size

In cases where standard effect size measures are difficult to define or construct from the information that is usually available in a published manuscript (e.g. multilevel models), we use the following approach to determine the sample size requirements for the replication attempt. The procedure outlined below assumes that the test statistic is normally distributed. In the last section of this document, we adapt the procedure to cover the t-distribution.
The relationship between the original sample size,
, and the standard error of the target estimate,
represents the contribution of study design elements (e.g., multistage sampling) and population parameters (variance of the focal predictor) that is separable from sample size.

is the target effect size obtained from a point estimate from the selected test result (e.g. logistic regression coefficient). Essentially, we are finding the standard error that we would need for the replication attempt in order to reject the null hypothesis at two-sided
. We provide some example R code below.

### This example involves multiple logistic regression analyses with two independent variables (a continuous predictor and a dichotomous experimental condition) as well their interaction. The selected test result involved the interaction term (From Table 2: B = -0.82, se = .31, p < 0.01, Odds ratio = 0.44).
N <- 225 ### sample size (reported in text of paper) ###
se.r <- uniroot(fun, interval = c(0.001,10), mu = obs.es, bound = 0.9)$root
(se.o/se.r)^2 #ratio of original sample size to replication sample size

means every element of the study design and target population that influences power is the same or similar across the original study and the replication attempt. For complex study designs and analyses, this could be a myriad of study elements and population parameters (e.g. intra-class correlation coefficients, number of observations per cluster, sample variance of the focal predictor(s), psychometric properties, etc.).
